_comment: Buyer archetype taxonomy. Two levels: organisational (stable property of the buying institution) and opportunity (property of the specific procurement event). Both assessed by AI with evidence.

organisational._comment: Assessed in the buyer intelligence dossier (Agent 2 Skill 2). Describes how the institution generally behaves as a buyer. Stable across procurements.

archetypes.name: Strategic Central Commissioner
archetypes.description: Mature commercial centre, strong governance, expects sophisticated outcome-led propositions.
archetypes.bidImplication: Play the process. Invest in understanding governance layers. Demonstrate strategic alignment, not just delivery capability.

archetypes.name: Framework-First Operator
archetypes.description: Buys through accessible routes where speed and compliant fit matter more than visionary repositioning.
archetypes.bidImplication: Framework position is the entry barrier. Win on fit, compliance, and mobilisation readiness.

archetypes.name: Devolved Capacity-Constrained Buyer
archetypes.description: Limited commercial bandwidth; clarity, low-friction compliance, and implementation confidence matter.
archetypes.bidImplication: Make it easy for them. Clear, simple propositions. Don't assume procurement sophistication.

archetypes.name: Programme-Led Transformer
archetypes.description: Driven by a specific transformation agenda; more open to flexible procedure, dialogue, and solution shaping.
archetypes.bidImplication: Lead with innovation and fresh thinking. Expect engagement and iteration. Demonstrate transformation capability.

archetypes.name: Incumbent-Locked Risk-Averse Buyer
archetypes.description: Market formally open, but practically difficult to unseat due to operational, data, security, or TUPE lock-in.
archetypes.bidImplication: If challenger: price the transition cost, address TUPE/data/continuity head-on. If incumbent: protect position, demonstrate continuous improvement.

archetypes.name: Politically Sensitive High-Scrutiny Buyer
archetypes.description: Procurement tone shaped by controversy, citizen impact, ministerial attention, or audit exposure.
archetypes.bidImplication: Assurance-heavy response. Demonstrate audit readiness, risk management, and sensitivity to public perception.

archetypes.name: Compliance Procurement Machine
archetypes.description: Highly process-led, evaluation-led, and defensible; less interested in narrative flourish, more in audit-proof quality.
archetypes.bidImplication: Answer the question asked. Score against the stated criteria. Don't over-invest in differentiation narrative.

opportunity._comment: Assessed per pursuit during qualification or capture planning. Describes the specific procurement event, not the institution. Uses buyer dossier as context but assesses the opportunity independently.

archetypes.name: Framework-Locked Speed Buy
archetypes.description: Route is framework-driven, timelines are tight, buyer primarily wants low-friction compliant mobilisation.
archetypes.bidImplication: Speed and compliance are everything. Demonstrate you can mobilise within the framework terms. Don't over-engineer the solution.

archetypes.name: Open Competition Recompete
archetypes.description: Opportunity is formally contestable and the route is genuinely open.
archetypes.bidImplication: Full competitive response required. Differentiation matters. Understand the incumbent but don't assume they win.

archetypes.name: Flexible Procedure Solution Shaping
archetypes.description: Buyer likely to use dialogue, iteration, negotiation, or multi-stage shaping. Competitive flexible procedure allows bespoke multi-stage design if set out in the notice and tender documents.
archetypes.bidImplication: Invest in solution design capability. Prepare for dialogue rounds. Be ready to iterate. Relationships and responsiveness matter more than the written submission alone.

archetypes.name: Incumbent-Protected Continuity Buy
archetypes.description: Market is nominally open but the economics of displacement are poor.
archetypes.bidImplication: If challenger: only pursue if you have a genuine transition advantage or the incumbent has visibly failed. If incumbent: protect, demonstrate value, propose continuous improvement.

archetypes.name: Compliance-Heavy Defensible Procurement
archetypes.description: Buyer's dominant behaviour is auditability and procedural defensibility.
archetypes.bidImplication: Answer every question precisely. Cross-reference requirements systematically. Compliance matrix is king. Narrative flair adds nothing.

archetypes.name: Price-Pressured Commodity Buy
archetypes.description: Differentiation is weak and commercial competitiveness dominates.
archetypes.bidImplication: Win on price. Minimise cost of delivery. Don't invest in elaborate solution differentiation — the buyer is buying a commodity.

archetypes.name: Transformation-Led Outcome Buy
archetypes.description: Buyer is genuinely seeking delivery outcomes, operating model change, or capability uplift.
archetypes.bidImplication: Lead with outcomes and transformation narrative. Demonstrate you understand their future state, not just their current requirements. Solution design is the differentiator.

archetypes.name: Ambiguous or Mixed
archetypes.description: Evidence is incomplete or conflicting. The procurement does not clearly fit a single archetype.
archetypes.bidImplication: Hedged approach. Cover multiple bases. Seek clarification through pre-market engagement or clarification questions. Revisit classification as more information emerges.
